text: We present a seventy-year-old male with a diagnosis of gastric cancer, a T-three lesion noted on EUS with a negative metastatic workup, underwent neoadjuvant chemotherapy with oncology, had a partial response, however continued to have obstructive symptoms and elected for surgery.

The surgeon dissects the greater omentum from the transverse colon, entering the lesser sac. The greater curvature of the stomach is mobilized. The right gastroepiploic vessel is dissected and transected using a vascular stapler. The right gastric artery is circumferentially dissected and sealed with a vessel sealer, followed by transection with a vascular load stapler. The postpyloric duodenum is circumferentially dissected and transected using a sixty-five blue load stapler.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect and remove the stomach and lymphatics up to the left gastric region. The left gastric vein is circumferentially dissected and clipped, followed by the left gastric artery, which is dissected at its root and transected with a vascular load stapler. The body of the stomach is transected at the level of the left gastric, removing approximately eighty percent of the stomach with a stapler, while some fundus remains supplied by short gastric arteries. The specimen is extracted through the gel port, and the proximal margin is sent for frozen section analysis, which returns negative.

The surgeon uses laparoscopic technique to create the jejuno-jejunostomy of the Roux-en-Y, running 50 centimeters of biliary pancreatic limb from the ligament of Treitz and 100 centimeters of Roux limb. The jejuno-jejunostomy is formed using a staple technique. Subsequently, robotic technique is employed to fashion the gastrogenostomy, bringing the Roux limb to the remnant stomach. The posterior walls are anastomosed with a blue load stapler, and the anterior wall is closed with a running 2-0 Vicryl suture, reinforced with a running 2-0 V-lock suture.

After the anastomosis was complete, an air leak test was performed and no air leak was noted. An upper GI series performed on post-operative day three was negative for any intra-abdominal process. The patient's hospital course was prolonged by an aspiration event leading to pneumonia. The final pathology showed a four point five centimeter pro-differentiated adenocarcinoma. Margins were free of tumor. Sixteen of fifty four nodes were positive.
